questions.text: I hold the view that the suffering of anyone, anywhere, deserves to be weighed as real and significant in my moral reasoning.
I pick up on tension in a room before anyone says a word.
I find that keeping my emotional reactions in check during conversations about pain allows me to evaluate the situation more accurately.
I notice that my body doesn't carry tension the way others describe.
I often cancel my own plans to help a friend, colleague, or family member.
I find that keeping things for myself when others need them makes me uncomfortable.
I tend to work on whatever grabs my attention rather than following a priority list.
I find it puzzling when people say one thing but apparently mean another.
I believe personal achievement is one of the most fulfilling experiences in life.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the experience that keeps my ethical life from becoming a matter of routine and forces it to remain a matter of active engagement.
I hold the view that decisive action is a form of respect for everyone's time.
I believe that someone needs to take charge, and it might as well be me.
I naturally listen first and speak after others have shared their thoughts.
I sense when someone is carefully managing how they are perceived.
I feel anxious when plans change unexpectedly, even if the change might be positive.
I keep my commitments organized in a single trusted system so nothing falls through the cracks.
I feel that my emotional thermostat is set higher than average.
I find that transitions between emotional states happen abruptly for me, not gradually.
